definitions.definition: Intellectual Property Rights (IPRs) refer to the rights given to persons over their creations. These rights give the creators an exclusive right over their intellectual creations, such as literary, musical, artistic, and industrial property, including inventions, designs, and scientific discoveries.

A patent is a form of intellectual property that gives the inventor a temporary monopoly over the invention. It is a grant that provides the right to exclude others from making, using, selling, or distributing the invention.

Copyright is a form of intellectual property that gives the creator of an original work, such as a literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work, the exclusive right to reproduce, distribute, and display the work, and to create derivative works.
A trademark is a distinctive sign, symbol, phrase, or logo that identifies a product or service and distinguishes it from others in the market.

Industrial design refers to the aesthetic and functional aspect of a product, such as its shape, color, and ornamentation. It is a form of intellectual property that protects the visual appearance of a product.
A geographical indication (GI) is a sign or symbol that identifies a product as originating from a specific geographical area, such as a region, city, or village.
A utility model is a type of intellectual property that protects innovative designs or improvements to existing products or processes.
Intellectual property refers to creations of the mind, such as inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, and symbols, names and images used in commerce.
Intellectual Property Rights (IPRs) refer to the rights given to persons over their creations.
A patentable invention is an invention that meets the requirements of novelty, non-obviousness, and industrial applicability.
Novelty refers to the requirement that an invention must be new and not obvious to a person skilled in the art.
Non-obviousness refers to the requirement that an invention must be significantly different from what existed before, and not obvious to a person skilled in the art.
Industrial applicability refers to the requirement that an invention must be capable of being made or used in any kind of industry.
Prior art refers to all existing knowledge, publications, or public disclosures that are relevant to a patent application.
A patent search is a process of searching existing patents and other prior art to determine if a patent application meets the requirements of novelty and non-obviousness.
A patent office is a government agency responsible for granting and administering patents.
A patent attorney is a lawyer who specializes in patent law and represents clients in patent applications and disputes.
Copyright infringement occurs when someone uses or distributes a copyrighted work without permission from the copyright owner.
Trademark infringement occurs when someone uses a trademark that is similar to an existing trademark, or uses a trademark in a way that is likely to confuse consumers.
Industrial design infringement occurs when someone uses or distributes a product with a design that is substantially similar to an existing design.
Geographical indication infringement occurs when someone uses a geographical indication in a way that is false or misleading.
